plainMeaning: In this section I admit every way I may have disrespected sacred knowledge — disturbing study, envying learners, soiling holy books or touching them with my feet, and neglecting funds set aside for learning.
recitation: tathā jñānācāre jñāna rahe āśātanā kīdhī hoy. paḍhatāṁ, guṇatāṁ antarāya kīdho hoy. macchara dharyo hoy. akṣarane thūṁka lagāḍyuṁ hoy. pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī, kāgale paga lagāḍyo hoy. khamāsamaṇa pākhe parivāḍī līdhī hoy. jñānadravya-taṇī sārasaṁbhāḷa kīdhī na hoy. aneruṁ e jñānācāra viṣe. ॥ 1 ॥

wordByWord.gloss: and also; likewise (opening the next confession-section)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit tathā 'so, thus, and', here a sectional connective carrying over from the previous tally.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the pivot from the eighty-five vow-atichāras into the five ācāras.

wordByWord.etymology: jñāna ('knowledge') + ācāra ('conduct'), locative -e; the first of the five ācāras.
wordByWord.notes: ācāra is a preface-glossary term; here it is the eight-limbed discipline governing study.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jñā- 'to know'.
wordByWord.notes: Object of the disrespect named next; 'jñāna rahe' = 'with respect to knowledge'.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati postpositional use of rah- 'to remain/abide', here adverbial 'as regards'.
wordByWord.notes: Links jñāna to āśātanā: 'disrespect shown toward knowledge'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ā-śātana 'cutting into, diminishing'; here the diminishing of something sacred by disregard.
wordByWord.notes: A technical term: deliberate or careless dishonoring of jñāna, deva, guru, or holy objects.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati kīdhuṁ, past participle of kar- 'to do' (Skt. kṛ-).
wordByWord.notes: Feminine -ī agrees with āśātanā.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati subjunctive of ho- 'to be' (Skt. bhū-).
wordByWord.notes: The recurring 'kīdhī/kīdho … hoy' = 'may possibly have done', the confessional modality of the whole sūtra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍh- 'to read, recite' (cf. Skt. paṭh-), present participle 'in the act of reading'.
wordByWord.notes: First of the study-pair paḍhatāṁ/guṇatāṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: while reciting/conning (repeating to fix in memory)
wordByWord.etymology: from guṇ- 'to repeat, tell over' (cf. Skt. guṇ- 'to count/repeat'), present participle.
wordByWord.notes: paḍhatāṁ (reading) and guṇatāṁ (memorizing-repetition) together name the whole act of study.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit antar-aya 'coming in between'; also the name of the karma-type that obstructs.
wordByWord.notes: Disturbing another's study both is an antarāya and binds antarāya/jñānāvaraṇīya karma.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati past participle of kar- 'to do'.
wordByWord.notes: Masculine -o agrees with antarāya.

wordByWord.etymology: subjunctive of ho- 'to be'.
wordByWord.notes: Repeated auxiliary of possibility.

wordByWord.gloss: envy; jealousy; grudging spite (toward a learner)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit macchara from Sanskrit matsara 'envy, selfish grudge'.
wordByWord.notes: Here specifically jñāna-matsara: resentment at another's learning or unwillingness to share it.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati past participle of dhar- 'to hold' (Skt. dhṛ-).
wordByWord.notes: Agrees with macchara (masc.).

wordByWord.etymology: subjunctive of ho-.
Sanskrit akṣara 'imperishable; syllable, letter' + dative -ne.
wordByWord.notes: akṣara denotes the sacred written word, treated as worthy of reverence.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati thūṁk 'saliva' (onomatopoeic root thū-).
wordByWord.notes: Refers to wetting the fingertip with saliva to turn manuscript pages — soiling the akṣara. Book spelling થૂંક preserved.

wordByWord.etymology: causative of lag- 'to be attached' (Skt. lag-), past participle neuter.
wordByWord.notes: Neuter -uṁ agrees with thūṁk.

wordByWord.etymology: subjunctive of ho-.
Sanskrit paṭṭa 'board, tablet' → Gujarati pāṭī.
wordByWord.notes: First of the six revered study-articles enumerated.

wordByWord.etymology: from Sanskrit pustikā 'small book' via Prakrit potthī.
wordByWord.notes: The bound or loose-leaf sacred text itself.

wordByWord.gloss: book-wrapper/cover (cloth or folder protecting the pothī)
wordByWord.etymology: uncertain; likely from a wrapping/enclosing root (cf. kavala 'enclosing'), a manuscript-protector.
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain; some editions take it as a manuscript-case or a marker-strip. Flagged in notes.

wordByWord.gloss: the folding book-stand (on which the pothī rests for reading)
wordByWord.etymology: from sthāpanā 'placing, setting' (Skt. sthā-) → Prakrit ṭhavaṇa; the X-shaped reading-stand.
wordByWord.notes: The same word-family as the sthāpanācārya stand; here the wooden reading-rest.

wordByWord.gloss: the rosary/bead-string for counting Navkār recitations
wordByWord.etymology: navkār ('the Navkār/Namaskāra mantra') + vāḷī ('string, garland'); navkārvāḷī.
wordByWord.notes: A sacred counting-mālā, hence not to be touched by the foot.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati kāgaḷ 'paper' (via Persian/Arabic kāghaḏ), locative -e.
wordByWord.notes: Extends reverence from formal scripture to any written paper.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pada 'foot' → Gujarati pag.
wordByWord.notes: Touching sacred articles with the foot is a paradigmatic āśātanā.

wordByWord.gloss: set; let touch (the foot to them)
wordByWord.etymology: causative of lag-, past participle masc. agreeing with pag.
subjunctive of ho-.

wordByWord.gloss: the khamāsamaṇa obeisance (the ritual prostration to the guru)
wordByWord.etymology: from kṣamā-śramaṇa 'forbearing ascetic', the salutation 'icchāmi khamāsamaṇo…'.
wordByWord.notes: A study-round should be begun only after offering this bow.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati pākhe 'without' (cf. Prakrit pakkha 'side'), privative postposition.
wordByWord.notes: Governs khamāsamaṇa: 'without the khamāsamaṇa'.

wordByWord.gloss: a round/turn of recitation or study-sequence
wordByWord.etymology: from Sanskrit paripāṭī 'order, regular sequence' → Prakrit parivāḍī.
wordByWord.notes: Taking up the study-cycle without the prescribed preliminary bow is the lapse confessed.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati past participle of le- 'to take' (Skt. lā-/labh-), fem. agreeing with parivāḍī.
subjunctive of ho-.

wordByWord.gloss: of the knowledge-endowment (funds/property dedicated to learning)
wordByWord.etymology: jñāna + dravya ('substance, fund') + taṇī, Old Gujarati genitive ('belonging to').
wordByWord.notes: jñāna-dravya = community resources earmarked for scriptures, copying, and teaching; parallel to deva-dravya.

wordByWord.etymology: sāra ('attending to') + saṁbhāḷ ('looking after', from sam-bhṛ/smṛ); a stock pairing.
past participle of kar-.
Sanskrit na 'not'.
wordByWord.notes: Negates the verb: 'has NOT cared for' — failure of stewardship is itself the fault here.

wordByWord.etymology: subjunctive of ho-.
wordByWord.notes: 'kīdhī na hoy' = 'may not have done'.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati aneruṁ 'other' (cf. Skt. anya 'other').
wordByWord.notes: The catch-all that sweeps up every unnamed lapse, as in every section's refrain.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati demonstrative e 'this/that'.
jñāna + ācāra.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the section by naming it again, as the formula requires.

wordByWord.etymology: from Sanskrit viṣaya 'sphere, domain', adverbial 'in the matter of'.
wordByWord.notes: 'aneruṁ e jñānācāra viṣe' = 'whatever else there is concerning this jñānācāra'.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral ૧ = 1, framed by daṇḍa bars.
wordByWord.notes: Numbers this as the first of the five-ācāra confessions; preserved verbatim per the book's punctuation.

literalTranslation: And, in the conduct of knowledge: if disrespect has been shown toward knowledge; if, while reading or memorizing, an obstacle has been caused; if envy has been harbored; if spittle has been applied to the letters; if the foot has been set upon the writing-slate, the scripture, the book-wrapper, the book-stand, the rosary, or paper; if a study-round has been taken up without the khamāsamaṇa bow; if the knowledge-endowment has not been cared for — and whatever else there may be concerning this conduct of knowledge. ॥1॥
idiomaticTranslation: Turning now to my conduct toward sacred knowledge: I confess every irreverence I may have shown it — interrupting another at study, nursing jealousy of a fellow learner, wetting the holy letters with spittle to turn a page, letting my foot touch a writing-board, a scripture, its wrapper or stand, my rosary or any written paper, taking up my round of study without first offering the khamāsamaṇa, and neglecting the funds entrusted to learning. For these and for any further lapse in the conduct of knowledge — micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having sealed the eighty-five vow-atichāras in the previous lines with the all-gathering micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ, the confession now opens its second movement: the five ācāras (right conduct in knowledge, faith, character, austerity, energy). This verse — numbered (1) afresh because the ācāra-tally restarts — audits the first, jñānācāra, the eight-limbed discipline of study (kāla, vinaya, bahumāna, upadhāna, aniṇhavaṇa, vyañjana, artha, tadubhaya). Rather than reciting those eight names, the text does what it does everywhere: it translates the discipline into vividly domestic failures. 'Disrespect (āśātanā) toward knowledge' heads the list as the master-fault. Then come three inward lapses — causing an antarāya (interruption) to someone reading or memorizing, and harbouring macchara (jealousy) of a learner — both of which the tradition warns bind jñānāvaraṇīya and antarāya karma, the very karmas that later starve one's own learning. The verse then turns to the body's careless contact with the sacred written word: moistening the akṣara with spittle to turn a page, and — most pointedly — letting the foot touch the six revered study-articles (pāṭī, pothī, kavalī, ṭhavaṇī, nokāravālī, and even ordinary paper). Two procedural lapses close it: beginning a parivāḍī (study-round) without the preliminary khamāsamaṇa, and failing to steward jñāna-dravya, the communal funds dedicated to scriptures and teaching. The closing 'aneruṁ e jñānācāra viṣe' leaves the section deliberately open, to be sealed (with the following darśanācāra in verse 2) by the shared refrain.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the doctrine that knowledge is not merely information but a sacrament whose mishandling has karmic weight. Each named lapse is a cause of jñānāvaraṇīya (knowledge-obscuring) and antarāya (obstructing) karma: to block another's study, to envy it, or to profane the written word is, by the law of karmic reciprocity, to dim one's own future jñāna. Reverence toward the physical vehicles of śruta — book, slate, stand, rosary, paper — embodies the aṅgas of vinaya (reverence) and bahumāna (honour) within jñānācāra, while care of jñāna-dravya extends the lay duty of guarding consecrated property (parallel to deva-dravya and the dravya-faults confessed earlier in the sūtra). The required khamāsamaṇa before a study-round encodes the aṅga of vinaya toward the guru-lineage through whom śruta descends.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a reciter today the verse becomes a concrete checklist: do not wet your fingers with saliva to turn pages of a sūtra or any printed sheet; never set books, a navkārvāḷī, a stand or even loose paper on the floor or touch them with your feet; never interrupt or belittle someone who is studying, and watch the subtler fault of resenting another's learning; begin study with the khamāsamaṇa; and treat any money or material given for scriptures, copying or teaching as a sacred trust to be maintained, not neglected.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.x — the atichāras of the vows and the framework of right knowledge-conduct
The eight aṅgas of jñānācāra (kāla, vinaya, bahumāna, upadhāna, aniṇhavaṇa, vyañjana, artha, tadubhaya)
crossReferences.source: Āvaśyaka/śrāvaka-ācāra tradition; Anchalgachchha Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit

crossReferences.text: Laghu Atiyār — the short Devasi/Rāi parallel of this same jñānācāra confession

sources.title: Pratikramana — confession and repentance in Jainism
sources.consultedFor: Confirming the six-āvaśyaka frame and the meaning of pratikramaṇa as 'returning from violation', within which this ācāra-confession sits.

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti)
sources.consultedFor: The classical doctrine of jñāna, the atichāra framework, and knowledge-obscuring (jñānāvaraṇīya) / antarāya karma that grounds the faults confessed here.

sources.title: Kayotsarga
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the ritual vocabulary (khamāsamaṇa obeisance, kāyotsarga) surrounding the confession in the Āvaśyaka sequence.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati verse text, its placement as the first of the five-ācāra confessions, and the verse-number restart at (1).

notes: The reading of 'કવલી' (kavalī) is uncertain: it is most plausibly a manuscript-wrapper/cover or protective folder among the study-articles, but some editions read it as a manuscript-case or page-marker; rendered as 'book-wrapper/cover' and flagged. 'ઠવણી' (ṭhavaṇī) is taken as the folding reading-stand (from sthāpanā), not the sthāpanācārya itself. 'પરિવાડી' (parivāḍī) is read as paripāṭī, a 'round/turn' of recitation or study-sequence. Book orthography is preserved verbatim, including થૂંક (long ū), નોકારવાલી (with -lī rather than -ḷī), and the restart of verse-numbering at ॥૧॥ for the ācāra section; per this edition 'atichāra' is spelled અતિયાર. The eight aṅgas of jñānācāra are supplied from the standard śrāvaka-ācāra framework as interpretive background, not as words present in the verse.
